content:
# Luke Chapter 5 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), PDF pages 90-91 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/LUK_005_page_001.jpg, LUK_005_page_002.jpg

---

**1** And it came to pass, while the crowd was pressing upon him and hearing the word of God, that he was standing beside the lake of Gennesaret,

**2** and he saw two boats standing beside the lake; but the fishermen had gone out from them and were washing the nets.

**3** And having entered into one of the boats, which was Simon's, he asked him to put out a little from the land. And having sat down, he taught the crowds from the boat.

**4** And when he ceased speaking, he said to Simon, "Put out into the deep and let down your nets for a catch."

**5** And Simon answered and said, "Master, having labored through the whole night, we caught nothing; but at your word I will let down the nets."

**6** And having done this, they enclosed a great multitude of fish, and their nets were breaking.

**7** And they signaled to their partners in the other boat to come and help them. And they came and filled both boats, so that they were sinking.

**8** And when Simon Peter saw it, he fell down at the knees of Jesus, saying, "Depart from me, for I am a sinful man, Lord."

**9** For astonishment had seized him and all who were with him at the catch of fish which they had taken,

**10** and likewise also James and John, sons of Zebedee, who were partners with Simon. And Jesus said to Simon, "Do not fear; from now on you will be catching men."

**11** And having brought the boats to land, they left everything and followed him.

**12** And it came to pass, while he was in one of the cities, behold, a man full of leprosy; and having seen Jesus, he fell on his face and besought him, saying, "Lord, if you are willing, you are able to cleanse me."

**13** And having stretched out his hand, he touched him, saying, "I am willing; be cleansed." And immediately the leprosy departed from him.

**14** And he charged him to tell no one, but, "Go, show yourself to the priest and offer for your cleansing just as Moses commanded, for a testimony to them."

**15** But the word concerning him spread all the more, and great crowds gathered to hear and to be healed from their infirmities.

**16** But he himself was withdrawing in the desolate places and praying.

**17** And it came to pass on one of the days, that he was teaching, and there were Pharisees and teachers of the law sitting by, who had come from every village of Galilee and Judea and Jerusalem; and the power of the Lord was present for him to heal.

**18** And behold, men carrying upon a bed a man who was paralyzed, and they were seeking to bring him in and set him before him.

**19** And not finding by what way they might bring him in on account of the crowd, they went up on the housetop and let him down through the tiles, with the small bed, into the midst before Jesus.

**20** And seeing their faith, he said, "Man, your sins are forgiven you."

**21** And the scribes and the Pharisees began to reason, saying, "Who is this who speaks blasphemies? Who is able to forgive sins except God alone?"

**22** But Jesus, having perceived their reasonings, answered and said to them, "Why do you reason in your hearts?

**23** Which is easier, to say, 'Your sins are forgiven you,' or to say, 'Rise and walk'?

**24** But that you may know that the Son of Man has authority on earth to forgive sins" — he said to the paralyzed man — "I say to you, rise, take up your bed, and go to your house."

**25** And immediately, having risen before them, he took up that upon which he had been lying, and went away to his house, glorifying God.

**26** And amazement seized all, and they glorified God, and they were filled with fear, saying, "We have seen extraordinary things today."

**27** And after these things he went out and saw a tax collector named Levi sitting at the tax booth, and he said to him, "Follow me."

**28** And having left everything, he rose and followed him.

**29** And Levi made a great feast for him in his house, and there was a great crowd of tax collectors and others who were reclining at table with them.

**30** And the Pharisees and their scribes were grumbling against his disciples, saying, "Why do you eat and drink with tax collectors and sinners?"

**31** And Jesus answered and said to them, "Those who are well have no need of a physician, but those who are sick.

**32** I have not come to call the righteous but sinners to repentance."

**33** And they said to him, "The disciples of John fast often and make prayers, and likewise also those of the Pharisees, but yours eat and drink."

**34** And Jesus said to them, "You are not able to make the sons of the bridechamber fast while the bridegroom is with them, are you?

**35** But days will come, and when the bridegroom is taken away from them, then they will fast in those days."

**36** And he spoke also a parable to them: "No one tears a piece from a new garment and puts it on an old garment; otherwise he will both tear the new, and the piece from the new will not match the old.

**37** And no one puts new wine into old wineskins; otherwise the new wine will burst the wineskins, and it will be poured out, and the wineskins will be destroyed.

**38** But new wine must be put into fresh wineskins.

**39** And no one having drunk old wine desires new, for he says, 'The old is good.'"

---

## LOGOS Translation Notes

### Manuscript Observations (Codex Sinaiticus)

1. **Page layout:** Luke 5 begins near the end of page 90 (folio 46r), column 1, at approximately verse 3, with the opening verses carried over from the preceding page. The chapter continues across all four columns of page 90 and into columns 1-2 of page 91 (folio 46v), where it concludes at verse 39. Columns 3-4 of page 91 begin Luke 6.

2. **Verse 1 — "lake of Gennesaret" (LIMNH GENNHCARET):** Sinaiticus reads LIMNH (lake) rather than QALACCA (sea). Luke consistently uses LIMNH for the body of water that Matthew and Mark call QALACCA. The LOGOS preserves Luke's distinct terminology.

3. **Verse 3 — "Simon's" (TOU SIMWNOC):** Sinaiticus identifies the boat as Simon's before the call narrative proper, establishing a prior connection between Jesus and Simon that Luke does not elaborate. The LOGOS retains this without addition.

4. **Verse 5 — "Master" (EPICTATΑ):** Sinaiticus reads EPICTATΑ, a term unique to Luke among the Gospels. It denotes one who has authority over others, rendered "Master" to distinguish it from KYRIE ("Lord"), which Peter uses later in verse 8. This shift in address reflects Peter's deepening recognition.

5. **Verse 8 — "Simon Peter" (SIMWN PETROC):** Sinaiticus reads the double name here, the first occurrence of "Peter" in Luke's narrative. The LOGOS preserves this as written, marking the transitional moment.

6. **Verse 12 — "full of leprosy" (PLHRHC LEPRAC):** This expression is unique to Luke among the Synoptics and may reflect medical vocabulary, indicating an advanced stage of the disease. The LOGOS preserves the phrase literally.

7. **Verse 14 — "just as Moses commanded" (KAQWC PROSETAXEN MWYCHC):** Sinaiticus preserves the reference to Leviticus 14:2-32, the prescribed purification ritual. The LOGOS renders KAQWC as "just as," maintaining the formal correspondence to the Mosaic legislation.

8. **Verse 17 — "teachers of the law" (NOMODIDASKALOI):** Sinaiticus reads NOMODIDASKALOI, a compound noun (NOMOC + DIDASKALOC) appearing only in Luke among the Gospels (also Acts 5:34; 1 Tim 1:7). Rendered "teachers of the law" to preserve the compound's transparent meaning, distinguishing this term from GRAMMATEIC ("scribes").

9. **Verse 19 — "through the tiles" (DIA TWN KERAMWN):** Sinaiticus reads DIA TWN KERAMWN, reflecting Luke's description of a tiled roof, characteristic of Greco-Roman architecture, in contrast to Mark's account of digging through a mud-and-thatch roof (Mark 2:4). The LOGOS preserves Luke's architectural detail without harmonization.

10. **Verse 20 — "Man" (ANQRWPE):** Sinaiticus reads ANQRWPE (vocative), a direct address rendered "Man." This is a distinctively Lukan form of address used by Jesus (cf. Luke 12:14; 22:58, 60), less intimate than "Son" (TEKNON) found in the Markan parallel (Mark 2:5).

11. **Verse 26 — "extraordinary things" (PARADOXA):** Sinaiticus reads PARADOXA, from which the English "paradox" derives. The term means "beyond expectation" or "contrary to what is supposed." Rendered "extraordinary things" to convey the sense of events that surpass ordinary understanding.

12. **Verse 32 — "to repentance" (EIC METANOIAN):** Sinaiticus includes the prepositional phrase EIC METANOIAN, which is absent from some manuscripts of the Markan parallel (Mark 2:17). This is characteristic of Luke's theological emphasis on repentance. The LOGOS retains it as read in Sinaiticus.

13. **Verse 36 — Parable of the garment:** Sinaiticus preserves Luke's distinctive form of this parable, which differs from Mark and Matthew. In Luke, the new garment is torn (not merely a patch of unshrunk cloth), and the piece from the new does not match the old. This Lukan form sharpens the incompatibility between old and new.

14. **Verse 39 — "The old is good" (O PALAIOC XRHCTOC ECTIN):** Sinaiticus reads XRHCTOC ("good, pleasant, kind") rather than XRHCTOTEPOC ("better") found in some later manuscripts. The LOGOS follows Sinaiticus. This saying, unique to Luke, may convey irony: those accustomed to the old ways naturally prefer them, yet this preference does not make the old superior.

### Translation Philosophy

- **"Besought" (EDEHQH):** Rendered with the more formal "besought" (v. 12) rather than "begged" or "asked," preserving the earnest, supplicatory force of DEOMAI, which in Luke consistently denotes intense petition directed toward a figure of authority.
- **"Reclining at table" (KATAKEIMENOI):** Rendered literally to preserve the cultural setting of the Greco-Roman banquet, where diners reclined on couches. This avoids the anachronistic "sitting at table."
- **"Sons of the bridechamber" (YIOUC TOU NYMFWNOC):** Retained as a literal rendering of the Semitic idiom (v. 34), denoting the wedding guests or attendants of the bridegroom. The expression is a Hebrew/Aramaic idiom (bene hachuppah) preserved in the Greek, and the LOGOS maintains its formal character rather than paraphrasing as "wedding guests."
- **"Tax collector" (TELWNHC):** Consistently rendered "tax collector" (vv. 27, 29, 30) rather than the archaic "publican." The TELWNHC was a local agent who collected indirect taxes (customs, tolls) on behalf of the Roman administration, a role that generated social stigma in Jewish society.
- **"Wineskins" (ACKOUC):** The Greek ACKOC refers to an animal skin used as a container for wine. Rendered "wineskins" throughout (vv. 37-38) to preserve the material reality of the image, essential to the parable's logic: old, dried skins lack the elasticity to contain fermenting wine.
